# Glossary

**Asset play** - stock where hidden or undervalued assets may be worth more than the market price reflects. (Modules 3, 6, 7)

**Balance sheet** - statement of assets, liabilities, and equity; central to survival analysis. (Module 6)

**Buyback** - company purchase of its own shares, reducing share count and increasing remaining owners' claim on earnings. (Modules 4, 6)

**Cash flow** - cash generated by a business; useful for asset plays and capital-intensive companies. (Module 6)

**Cyclical** - company whose earnings rise and fall with economic or industry cycles. (Modules 3, 8)

**Debt factor** - Lynch's emphasis on debt level, debt structure, and whether creditors can force bad outcomes. (Modules 6, 7)

**Diworseification** - Lynch's term for value-destroying diversification into unrelated or worse businesses. (Module 4)

**Fast grower** - company with rapid earnings expansion and large upside if growth persists. (Modules 3, 5, 8)

**Final checklist** - category-specific questions used before buying and while reviewing a stock. (Module 7)

**Hidden asset** - asset not fully recognized by the market, such as real estate, subsidiaries, brands, or media properties. (Module 6)

**Inventory buildup** - warning sign when inventory grows faster than sales, especially in retail or manufacturing. (Module 6)

**Market capitalization** - share price multiplied by shares outstanding. (Module 5)

**Mirror test** - investor self-check about finances, time horizon, and temperament before buying stocks. (Module 1)

**Niche** - protected competitive position that makes profits harder for rivals to attack. (Module 4)

**P/E ratio** - price-to-earnings ratio; useful only when judged against growth, quality, and category. (Modules 5, 6)

**PEG-style reasoning** - comparing P/E to sustainable earnings growth. (Modules 5, 7)

**Slow grower** - mature company with low growth, often dividend-oriented. (Modules 3, 8)

**Spinoff** - separated business that may be ignored or sold mechanically by investors after distribution. (Module 4)

**Stalwart** - large, dependable company with moderate growth and moderate upside. (Modules 3, 8)

**Story** - plain-language investment thesis explaining why earnings should improve. (Modules 5, 7)

**Street lag** - delay before Wall Street recognizes changes visible to consumers, workers, or local observers. (Module 1)

**Tenbagger** - stock that rises tenfold. (Module 2)

**Turnaround** - troubled company whose stock depends on repair, survival, and renewed profitability. (Modules 3, 6, 8)

**Two-minute drill** - concise articulation of the stock category, thesis, numbers, and risks. (Module 5)

**Whisper stock** - speculative idea promoted by rumor or excitement instead of fundamentals. (Module 4)
